metadata.summary: Schema-first GraphQL with SDL as source of truth, codegen-driven types, typed mutation payloads, per-request DataLoader for lists, and field-level authorization.

content.title: GraphQL API Design
content.summary: Schema-first GraphQL design where the SDL is the source of truth, codegen drives client and server types, and every mutation returns a typed MutationNamePayload with a userErrors array. Core rule: every list-returning resolver must use a DataLoader instantiated per-request; every field with sensitive data must have explicit field-level authorization.

why.p: Schema-first with codegen eliminates type drift between client and server. DataLoader prevents N+1 query explosions — the canonical GraphQL performance failure. Typed mutation payloads surface business errors structurally rather than as exceptions, enabling partial-success handling that REST cannot express. Query depth and cost limits prevent DoS from malicious nested queries.

list.item: Multiple clients (web + iOS + Android + partner) with divergent field needs over the same domain
Highly relational data where REST would force N+1 round-trips or sprawling ?expand= params
Schema-first development where the contract is the source of truth and codegen drives types
Subscriptions for live dashboards, chat, or collaborative editing
Federated services (Apollo Federation, GraphQL Hive) with one supergraph over many teams
Public cache-heavy CDN-fronted APIs — REST + HTTP cache headers wins
File upload or streaming binary payloads — multipart REST or signed URLs are simpler
Tiny CRUD app with a single client — schema overhead and N+1 traps are not worth it
Latency-sensitive RPC inside a cluster — gRPC is faster and stricter
When your team has zero GraphQL experience and the deadline is tight; learning curve hits hard at the resolver-and-dataloader stage

section.name: schema-design

rule.statement: Treat schema.graphql (SDL) as the source of truth. Run codegen in CI and fail if generated files drift from the committed schema.
rule.rationale: SDL-first ensures the API contract is reviewed before any implementation. Committing generated files by hand allows them to diverge from the schema silently.

rule.statement: Every root type should implement Node (with id: ID!) and Timestamped (with createdAt, updatedAt). This enables consistent caching and relay compliance.
rule.rationale: Relay client caching is keyed by id. Without a consistent id field, clients cannot normalize the cache. Timestamps are universally needed for auditing and display.

rule.statement: Every mutation must return a typed payload: MutationNamePayload containing the result object and a userErrors: [Error!]! array. Never throw exceptions for business errors.
rule.rationale: HTTP always returns 200 for GraphQL. Exceptions become opaque errors[] entries. Typed payloads allow clients to handle business errors (validation, conflicts) structurally in the same type system as success responses.

example.title: Node interface, Timestamped, and mutation payload

rule.statement: Use Relay cursor connections for every list field that could grow beyond 10 items. Never expose offset/limit pagination in the public schema.
rule.rationale: Offset pagination is unstable when items are inserted or deleted mid-page. Cursor pagination returns consistent results regardless of concurrent modifications. Relay clients (and many non-Relay clients) depend on the PageInfo structure for infinite scroll.

section.reference: Full reference schema with Connection types and mutation payloads

section.name: resolvers

rule.statement: Create a typed context dataclass per request containing current_user_id, loaders, and services. Pass it to every resolver via info.context.
rule.rationale: Resolver isolation requires all dependencies to flow through context, not module-level globals. A per-request context prevents cross-request state pollution (especially DataLoaders which must not be shared).

example.title: Strawberry context pattern with mutation error handling

rule.statement: Instantiate DataLoaders in the per-request context factory. Each request gets its own loader instances so batching is scoped to the request lifecycle.
rule.rationale: A process-level DataLoader accumulates keys from all concurrent requests, producing incorrect results by mixing data across users and requests.

section.reference: Strawberry DataLoader batch_load_fn with key-to-result mapping

rule.statement: Apply authorization at the field resolver level using permission classes, not only at the gateway or query root. A field added to a type inherits no auth from its parent.
rule.rationale: GraphQL composition allows clients to embed any field in any query. A gateway-level auth check on the top-level query does not propagate down to nested fields added later.

example.title: Strawberry permission classes

rule.statement: Add query depth limiting (max 10) and complexity analysis before production. Set a hard max on pagination first arguments server-side.
rule.rationale: A friends.friends.friends... query triggers exponential DB calls. Without server-side pagination caps, a client requesting first: 1000000 bypasses the design and runs a full table scan.

example.title: Strawberry depth limiter and complexity extension
example.code:
from strawberry.extensions import QueryDepthLimiter from strawberry.extensions.query_complexity import QueryComplexityExtension
schema = strawberry.Schema( query=Query, mutation=Mutation, extensions=[ QueryDepthLimiter(max_depth=10), QueryComplexityExtension(max_complexity=1000), ], )
# Server-side pagination cap in resolver @strawberry.field async def users(self, info: Info, first: int = 20) -> UserConnection: if first > 100: raise ValueError("Cannot request more than 100 items") ...

antipattern.description: Resolver eagerly loads all related entities in the parent query, regardless of whether the client requested them.
antipattern.reason: Executes DB queries for data the client never uses, degrading performance proportionally to the number of unused relations loaded.

antipattern.description: List field returns all items with no pagination: async def users(self) -> List[User].
antipattern.reason: Returns millions of rows in one request on a large dataset. All list fields that could grow must use cursor-based Connection types with server-enforced size caps.

example.title: N+1 without DataLoader
Batched via DataLoader

antipattern.description: Mutations raise exceptions for business errors (validation, not found, conflict) instead of returning typed error payloads.
antipattern.reason: Exceptions become opaque errors[] entries with no type information. Clients cannot distinguish "user not found" from "permission denied" without string-matching error messages.

antipattern.description: Schema breaking changes (renaming or removing a field) merged without a deprecation cycle and schema diff check in CI.
antipattern.reason: Clients that query the renamed/removed field receive a GraphQL error. Breaking changes require @deprecated(reason: "..."), usage analytics to confirm zero traffic, then removal.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: Schema-first: write or evolve schema.graphql first; agents then run codegen for typed resolvers (graphql-codegen, strawberry, ariadne-codegen) and stub each missing resolver. A second agent audits the schema for N+1 risk (any list field that returns objects with their own resolvers) and inserts DataLoader scaffolding. A third agent generates persisted-query manifests for production. Always gate schema breaking changes behind graphql-inspector diff in CI; never auto-merge a breaking change.

Recommended subagents: faion-sdd-executor-agent drives the schema → resolver → test loop one type at a time. A custom graphql-schema-reviewer (created under agents/) reads only schema.graphql + diff and produces breaking-change reports.
section.prompt: You are extending schema.graphql. Add type <X> with fields <...>. Constraints: - Use Relay-style Connection for any list field returning >10 items. - Every nullable field must have a justification comment. - Output a breaking-change diff against current schema using graphql-inspector format.

For resolver <Type.field>, write a DataLoader-batched implementation. Inputs: <parent type>, batch by <key>, fetch via <repo method>. Output: resolver fn + dataloader registration + 1 unit test asserting N+1 collapses to 1 DB call.

section.name: cli-tools
section.p: graphql-codegen: TS/Go/Python type + resolver codegen — npm i -D @graphql-codegen/cli · the-guild.dev
graphql-inspector: Schema diff, breaking-change CI gate — npm i -D @graphql-inspector/cli
Apollo Rover: Federated supergraph composition + checks — curl -sSL https://rover.apollo.dev/nix/latest | sh
spectaql / magidoc: Static schema docs site — npm
graphqurl / gq: curl-equivalent for GraphQL endpoints — npm
strawberry-graphql: Python schema-first lib (codegen via decorators) — pip install strawberry-graphql
ariadne-codegen: Python typed client codegen — pip install ariadne-codegen
genql: TS typed client codegen from schema — npm
GraphQL Armor: Security middleware (depth, cost, alias limits) — escape.tech/graphql-armor

section.name: security-best-practices
section.p: Use Relay-style Connection (edges, pageInfo, opaque cursor) for every list >10 items; never offset/limit in public schema.
DataLoader per request, not per process; keys identity-equal to the actual fetch key (don't pre-stringify).
Make every mutation return a typed MutationNamePayload with a userErrors: [Error!]! array — never throw for business errors.
Persisted queries / Automatic Persisted Queries in production; reject unknown query hashes — kills introspection-based DoS.
Disable introspection in prod or gate it behind admin auth; tools like Apollo Studio can ingest the schema via uploads instead.
Field-level auth via a single policy layer (e.g., graphql-shield, custom directive @auth(role: "ADMIN")); never sprinkle if (user.role !== ...) in resolvers.
Track the supergraph in a registry; CI runs inspector diff and fails on breaking changes unless tagged BREAKING_CHANGE_APPROVED.

section.name: ai-agent-gotchas
section.p: LLMs forget DataLoader. They will write users.map(u => fetchUserOrders(u.id)) and ship N+1s. Force "every list-returning resolver MUST use a dataloader" in the prompt.
Agents tend to expose password, email, apiKey on the User type. Provide an explicit "fields to never expose" list.
Generated mutations often throw new Error("not found") instead of returning userErrors. Provide the MutationPayload template.
Schema diffs from agents may rename fields rather than deprecate; require @deprecated(reason: "...") for any removal.
Subscriptions written by agents commonly leak DB connections / forget to unsubscribe on disconnect. Add a checklist item: unsubscribe + cleanup is asserted in test.
Cost limits: an agent will set maxCost: 1_000_000 to "make tests pass". Lock the value and require a justification comment if changed.
Human-in-loop checkpoint: any change to schema.graphql that triggers an inspector BREAKING result must have a human reviewer.

example.title: GraphQL Armor plugins

section.name: services
section.p: Apollo GraphOS / Studio (SaaS, Rover CLI + REST API): Schema registry, checks, federation; agents drive via rover subgraph publish.
GraphQL Hive (OSS + SaaS, CLI + API): OSS alternative to Apollo Studio.
Hasura (OSS + SaaS, hasura CLI + metadata files): Auto-generated GraphQL over Postgres; metadata is YAML, agent-friendly.
PostGraphile (OSS): Postgres → GraphQL with row-level security.
Stepzen / IBM API Connect (SaaS): Schema stitching as a service.
AWS AppSync (SaaS, CDK / Terraform): Managed GraphQL with VTL/JS resolvers; works well in IaC pipelines.
Apollo Server (OSS): De-facto Node server; integrates with most tracing stacks.
Yoga / The Guild (OSS): Lightweight alt to Apollo Server, modern envelop plugins.

template.purpose: Full reference schema: scalars, interfaces, Connection types, Query/Mutation/Subscription roots
Strawberry DataLoader batch_load_fn with key-to-result mapping
